data.title: International Airlines Group: Downgraded To Buy On Fuel Risk
data.description:
International Consolidated Airlines Group (ICAGY) (BABWF) stock price has increased nearly 60% since my last report, which is extremely close to my price target set for 2025.

In my prior report, I already noted the geopolitical risk, and we now see that playing out:

While IAG has been able to efficiently extract value from the market, we do note that geopolitical tension such as war and trade may somewhat pressure the strong ability to extract value from the market efficiently.

Given that there is some uncertainty in the markets due to the situation in Iran, which does affect oil prices, which is indirectly one of the major input costs for airlines, I believe it is a good moment to revisit investment prospects of IAG, as the stock has now risen 265% since I initiated coverage with a buy rating in July 2022 and uncertainty is higher than in the years prior.

Jet Fuel Prices Head Higher

The main risk for IAG is the same as for other airlines, as I outlined in a separate report. Higher oil prices are a near-direct pressure on airline margins, while any softening in demand, either through macro conditions shifting or fear of flying, could also soften unit revenues. Bloomberg has reported that jet fuel prices surged nearly 50% in Europe in just two days. It quite well shows the risk that airlines may face on their financials.

While US airlines have largely stepped away from jet fuel hedging. IAG is still doing it as discussed in their earnings release:

The Group seeks to reduce the impact of volatile commodity prices by hedging prices in advance. The Group’s fuel hedging policy is designed to provide flexibility to respond to both significant unexpected reductions in travel demand or capacity and/or material or sudden changes in jet fuel prices. The policy allows for differentiation within the Group, to match the nature of each operating company, and the use of call options for a proportion of the hedging undertaken. The policy operates on a three-year rolling basis, with hedging of up to 75% of anticipated requirements in near-term, reducing steadily over the first two years down to 20% for the final year. The policy also provides flexibility for low-cost airlines within the Group to adopt hedging of up to 80% in the near-term.

The hedging policy is to typically be up to 75% hedged for the first year and even 80% for low-cost airlines, and as we see with basically all hedging policies, outyears fuel hedging as a percentage of expected usage are lower, towards 20% in year three. So, there is an impact, but only on around 25% of the fuel costs. Effectively that would mean that fuel costs currently are around 11.7% higher against the undisturbed jet fuel price. However, the company had 62% hedged, meaning the impact is almost 18%.

Lower Fuel Costs Drove IAG Earnings In 2025

In 2025, IAG’s revenues grew 3.5%, including 2.5% growth in passenger revenues and 16% growth in other revenues. We note that passenger revenues increased in line with the 2.4% capacity expansion, showing that unit revenues were more or less stable. Passenger revenues were still the main growth driver of revenues, but it is evident that there hasn’t been any upside on unit revenues. Other revenues drove nearly 40% of the growth as loyalty program, maintenance, and ground handling revenues increased.

Operating profit increased 13.1%, or €581 million, driven by a €525 million decrease in fuel costs. Putting it differently, 90% of the profit increase was driven by lower fuel costs, and that does show the pressure for airlines even with fuel hedges in place.

Currently, it is extremely difficult to assess what the impact of higher prices will be, as we need to see for how long prices will remain elevated. However, if we were to apply an 18% increase in blended fuel cost for 2025, we would see that operating profit would be €3.75 billion, driving a 380-basis point margin pressure. So this quite well shows the sensitivity of margins to jet fuel prices.

IAG Downgraded To Buy, But There May Be Upside

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

The stock price target is based on the listing of IAG at the London Stock Exchange converted to USD, as the home listing provides better volumes, allowing for better price making and buying and selling at desired prices and quantities. Other ways to invest in International Airlines Group are through the OTC tickers ICAGY (representing two ordinary shares) and BABWF (representing one ordinary share).

While I would like to be able to say that we can model the impact of higher fuel prices. The reality is that we cannot. There is simply too much unknown to be able to make a fully informed estimate on it. However, we can model a 3-point drag to the margins and see what that does for the valuation case of IAG. We note that the assumption is the roughly four-point impact we noted against 2025 earnings minus one point of recovery that is typically possible.

This has also been cross-verified through calculations with IAG’s guidance already assuming a €400 million impact solely from the curve shifting between the end of 2025 and the reporting date. The average hedged fuel price was $751 per metric ton. The new fuel price is roughly $449 per metric ton higher. This brings the blended price to $922 per metric ton. Converting to euros and multiplying by the fuel volume that would drive a €1.7 billion impact with a net of a 40% recovery through pass-through would be €1 billion and around €0.9 billion, as roughly two months have already been executed in 2026. That would bring a roughly 280 basis points headwind, which I believe can be rounded to 3 points. Due to lower hedging for 2027, the impact for 2027 would be more than twice as large.

If we implement that into the price targets, we note that there is 9% downside from current prices and 29% downside for the stock price target for 2027. However, I believe that the surge to $1,200 may not stick. A more realistic scenario in my view may be that the price goes to $1,000 or even $900.

If fuel prices go to $1,000, we see that upside will be around 7%, with 2027 offering little to no upside against the projected target for 2026.

If we set the prices at $900, there remains around 12% upside for 2026 and 21% upside for 2027. I believe this does provide the more balanced scenario, and I set a price target of $5.59 or $11.18 per ICAGY share (presenting two ordinary shares), providing 12% upside and $6.01 or $12 with forward pricing.

The risk is obviously that fuel prices remain elevated. However, I do believe that the scenario of a $900 target in the near term is more reflective of reality. While my monitor does show a strong buy rating at $900 per metric ton. I do feel the uncertainty warrants a downgrade from strong buy to buy.

Conclusion: Fuel Price Is Definitely A Risk But I Do Not Expect Current High Prices To Stick

Fuel prices can definitely erode margins, and more often than not, that is why airline stocks are volatile. High fuel prices erode margins more than they create revenue opportunities, while very low fuel prices may be signs of economic distress that can affect the top line. Airlines benefit from a situation somewhere in between where oil prices are low but not low due to a fundamentally challenging macro picture that may affect travel demand. For IAG, I do believe that there is indeed a risk, but I also do not believe that the current fuel price situation will stick. Therefore, I do downgrade the stock to buy, but I currently do not see a strong reason to downgrade to hold or even sell.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 2 Retirement Yield Picks That Give You The Right To Win
data.description:
In management consulting, which is my background, there is a common question that consultants and corporate leaders try to address together before embarking on new capital allocation:

Does your strategy give you the right to win?

It is a holistic question, which forces one to take both a helicopter view (geography, sector, market share) and deep-dives (various capabilities, balance sheet, product) to understand whether the business stands a good chance to deliver superior long-term returns by doing what it has been doing so far or by venturing into new areas.

The idea is that businesses have to earn their right to win.

It can be earned by making choices that are aligned with capabilities/strengths - i.e., "playing on your strengths".

Now, how does this fit together with durable and/or retirement income investing? Here are my thoughts.

In durable income investing the concept of winning is a combination of

a) stable current income streams,

b) tangible yields,

c) compounding at a meaningful tempo.

When thinking about whether the investment strategy gives us the right to win, we have to earn it by aligning our choices (picks) with the businesses that have durable cash flows, predictability and smooth compounding (growth) as their strengths.

Plus, we have to make sure that these items are really their strengths and not temporary features due to, say, favorable market conditions or the right cycle.

The recent equity CLO (OXLC), weaker private credit (FSCO) and aggressive yield covered call ETF product (ULTY) NAV (and yield) declines serve as good proof. And this can also happen in optically de-risked segments of the market like investment grade telcos (T) and equity REITs (ARE).

Let me now present two investment picks, which, in my view, embody all of the critical elements that should give you, as a durable income investor, the right to win (i.e., compounding cash flows in a smooth and stress-free fashion).

Two 5%+ yield picks that give you the right to win in your retirement portfolio

Effectively, the notion of winning is about maximizing yield and its growth without experiencing value/income-destructive bumps in the process.

Enbridge Inc. (ENB) - midstream infrastructure - and Main Street Capital Corporation (MAIN) - a business development company/private credit vehicle - have what it takes.

So, let's start with the first and the easiest aspect, which is the yield.

ENB and MAIN provide a dividend yield of 5.2% and 7.3%, respectively. These are not sky-high levels, but definitely something that does not introduce a drag on income compounding.

Now, and this is where the substance lies, the underlying fundamentals are so robust that the yield per unit of risk is truly attractive.

Both players have managed to distribute consistent dividend for decades and across different market regimes, which I think could be treated as a positive sign:

Neither ENB nor MAIN have cut their dividends since their IPO dates, which has happened before the GFC. Instead, both businesses have gradually expanded the distributions, while delivering juicy price appreciation.

In the chart, looking at MAIN, we will notice some periodic spikes (both to the upside and downside). These are special distributions that go over base dividends. Usually, when the market conditions go south, MAIN tends to trim down special dividends in order to safeguard its balance sheet. Conversely, when it manages to monetize some of its equity investment portfolio, it rewards its income investors, accordingly.

Here we can see how huge role the special dividends have played for MAIN (just look at MAIN's total return vs. its price return):

Having said that, ENB and MAIN offer something much more important and valuable in the context of durable dividend generation than just almost perfect history.

For ENB the case is very straightforward. It runs a utility-like business, where the cash flows are underpinned by mostly long-term fee-based and inflation-linked contracts, which are stipulated by IG grade offtakers.

And the infrastructure we are talking about here is gas transmission, gas storage, gas distribution, liquid pipes and renewable power plants, which almost all are backed by long-term power purchase agreements. Positioning-wise ENB's portfolio is constructed almost perfectly, allowing the shareholders to participate in the AI expansion through the reliable baseload power angle.

The fact that ENB pays out only 60% to 70% of distributable cash flows and have the organic growth supported by the fee-based contracts, which provide close to 2% annual income growth create a favorable ground for organic long-term compounding.

For MAIN, the business model does not look as great as for ENB, at least not on the surface. It is a private credit machine, which already is enough to scare away a large chunk of investors.

However, the devil is in the details. And right because of these details, MAIN has been able to deliver consistent dividends without NAV erosion for about 2 decades in a row.

And here I would like to underscore 5 points, which are vital to neutralize some unjustified biases around MAIN:

First, MAIN has a diversified portfolio with no notable exposure to SaaS, which has been the key driver for the recent BDC (BIZD) selloff:

Second, MAIN invests in lower middle market companies, where there is weaker competition from the private credit giants such as KKR (KKR) and Blue Owl (OWL) simply because of the scale issue. As a result of this, MAIN can access greater demand from which to cherry pick the right borrowers, while being strict on the covenant side.

Third, MAIN's portfolio companies carry conservative capital structures and are far away from being speculative. For example, on average, the senior leverage of its portfolio company stands only at 2.4x. The EBITDA to senior interest coverage is at 3.0x. These are one of the strongest metrics in the BDC arena.

Fourth, MAIN injects equity capital along with its private credit finance in order to secure some equity-like upside potential. Once these stakes get monetized, MAIN can usually capture sizeable returns, which can both offset credit losses and accommodate juicy special distributions (as I indicated above). For instance, as of Q4, 2025, MAIN had ~$7.10 per share in cumulative, pre-tax net unrealized appreciation, which corresponds to ~12% of its market cap.

Fifth, MAIN has the third least leveraged balance sheet in the BDC world. The debt to equity is 0.73x, while the sector average is a bit over 1.2x, which is also the optimal level of debt for BDCs (i.e., it maximizes ROE without entering aggressive risk zone).

Final words

To earn the right to win or achieve great and durable income compounding results, our portfolios have to be based on assets that carry tangible entry yields, defensive fundamentals and income growth prospects.

In other words, these three have to be core strengths of their (portfolio asset) investment offering. The third aspect - income growth - is, however, a bit more nuanced as sometimes it might make sense to accept a below-inflation-rate income growth rate as long as it comes with higher entry yield, which, in turn, offers greater flexibility to make reinvestments and thus compound the income that way.

In my view, ENB and MAIN are definitely solid retirement income picks, which give us the right to win in the compounding journey.

data.title: BJ's Wholesale Club: Investing In Expansion, EPS Growth To Slow
data.description:
In our last column in the fall of 2025, we upgraded BJ's Wholesale Club Holdings, Inc. (BJ) to a Buy following a valuation reset and the projected growth. Shares were able to be grabbed at $87-$88 on the day of publication and rallied to over $102 in a few weeks. That is a solid trading return. Shares were a bit sideways since then but have dipped back toward $95 following the just-reported earnings, which we will discuss in this update column. What has changed is we have 2026 guidance, and that calls for pretty slow growth.

We think BJ shares are a Hold right now but would be compelled to move to a Buy once again if we retrace to the mid-$80s, where valuation relative to expected growth would make more sense. Let us discuss.

BJ's Wholesale Club Q4 Top-Line Figures And Margin Performance

Comparable sales are the key metric we watch for in retailers. Of course we also monitor total sales and margins, looking for expansion on both fronts. We like holding a current position as metrics are all healthy, but the growth is slow. Sure, the valuation is stretched for the growth, but it is a modest premium trading at a market multiple. We saw another quarter of year-over-year sales growth for BJ's, with revenues increasing 5.7% to $5.58 billion. And this surpassed consensus estimates slightly by $30 million. That is welcome news for longs. But more importantly, comparable sales were positive.

We want to reiterate that you should be aware that comparable sales here are influenced by fuel and gas prices, but the main driver is still merchandise margins and the price-to-sales mix. Including gasoline, total comparable club sales were up by 1.6% year-over-year, which is nice growth. Backing out the volatile fuel sales, comparable sales were up a strong 2.6%. We also saw great online comparable sales growth that was up 31% year-over-year.

The other thing we like about the model is that people need to pay just to walk in the doors. That is right. Not only does BJ's make money on its sales, of course, but to get in the door you need a membership. And membership continues to grow, with membership fee income rising 10.9%. Over 90% of existing members renewed in 2025 as well.

Then there are the margins, which we hone in on after examining total sales and comparable sales. These are showing strength as well and justify a buy, particularly on weakness in shares. It was a solid 5.5% increase in gross profit to $1.01 billion from $949.0 million compared to last year's Q4.

One blemish was that the merchandise gross margin rate (which backs out fuel sales and membership fee income) slipped by 50 basis points, but this was due to promotions and the sales mix. For the whole year, margins on merchandise were flat, which is fine. We next look to selling and administrative expenses. When revenues rise, we always expect these to rise. Well, they were up YOY $818.2 million versus $758.2 million a year ago. Keep in mind, though, that this still reflects new store openings. How did this translate to earnings power?

First, we should note that we saw a slight increase in the adjusted EBITDA line. It rose a paltry 0.7% to $266.5 million versus $264.6 million last year. So, here, clearly, it is slow growth, so we can understand why near-term investors are not rushing to buy. Net income was up to $125.9 million from $122.7 million, also due to the rising expenses. But these are investments for future growth. EPS, though, was a nice beat of $0.03, coming in at $0.96, up from $0.92. For the year, EPS was $4.38, a solid rise from $4.00.

Forward View

Why are shares down? We think it is the outlook, particularly for earnings. The projections are for stable but slow growth. And so, the Street may be reluctant to pay a market multiple right now at about $95 per share.

As we look ahead to 2026, that all-important comparable club sales metric (backing out fuel sales) is expected to increase 2.0% to 3.0% year-over-year. But with a high capex of $800 million, it is going to weigh on near-term earnings. We have to stress, however, that this is to generate future growth. A lot of that money is going toward new club openings and enhancements across the distribution network.

From an EPS standpoint, the $4.50 in expected EPS is only $0.12 per share growth for the year, or less than 3%. As such, we like a Hold here at these levels. We expect higher sales, positive comps, and increased membership, but for now the market multiple is still a bit rich for this level of growth.

data.title: LIT: Lithium And Battery Technology Remains Bullish (Downgrade)
data.description:
I rated the Global X Lithium & Battery Tech ETF (LIT) a buy in a May 21, 2025, Seeking Alpha article, where I concluded with the following:

Any investment in the LIT ETF should leave plenty of room to add on further declines, as it's virtually impossible to pick bottoms in any market. Meanwhile, prices tend to extend when searching for bottoms, making a longer-term scale-down approach to LIT optimal.

LIT was trading at $38.18 per share on May 20, 2025, and was in a bearish trend, making lower highs and lower lows since the November 2021 high of $97.13 per share. LIT shares more than doubled at the most recent February 2026 high of $78 per share, but the shares ran out of upside steam as EV sales and the Iran war weighed on lithium and battery metals prices. I had a buy rating on the LIT ETF in late May 2025 and am downgrading it to a hold in early March 2026. At $70 per share, LIT is still over 80% higher than the price on May 20, 2025.

Lithium And Battery Metal Demand Are Rising

Lithium is the lightest known metal with high electrochemical potential and low density. The leading modern use is in the production of high-performance batteries, but it also has applications in metallurgy, nuclear physics, and chemical synthesis.

Pure lithium metal serves as the anode in non-rechargeable batteries used for long-life devices such as pacemakers, watches, and cameras. Most lithium-ion batteries use lithium compounds rather than pure lithium, but next-generation lithium-metal batteries require pure lithium-metal anodes to achieve the highest possible energy density. Lithium is a critical ingredient in the lithium batteries for electric vehicles (EVs), phones, and grid storage.

The chart shows that Australia is the world’s leading lithium producer, followed by Chile and China.

A January 14, 2026, article in Carbon Credits forecasted strong lithium demand and higher prices in 2026. Lithium carbonate spot prices in China have climbed from $8,259 on June 23, 2025, to over $13,000 per ton in late November 2025. In March 2026, the spot price of battery-grade lithium was around $20,000 per ton. While EV demand may have declined over the past year, demand for energy storage systems for AI has soared.

As the chart shows, the lithium supply-demand equation has flipped from a surplus in 2025 to a deficit in 2026. The deficit is forecast to substantially rise over the coming decade. Meanwhile, demand for all battery metals will likely grow as AI energy demands increase over the coming years.

LIT Is A Lithium And Battery Technology ETF

The fund profile for the Global X Lithium & Battery Tech ETF (LIT) states:

At $69.20 per share, LIT had $1.65 billion in assets under management and trades an average of over 386,000 shares per day, making it a liquid ETF. LIT charges a 0.75% management fee, and its blended annual dividend of $0.31 per share yields 0.44%.

RIO Is LIT's Top Holding

The top holdings of the LIT ETF include:

My May 21, 2025, Seeking Alpha article on LIT highlighted the Rio Tinto (RIO)-Codelco joint venture developing a high-grade lithium project at Chile’s Salar de Maricunga. Chile is the world’s second-leading lithium-producing country. In late May, LIT’s leading holding was Albemarle Corporation (ALB), a U.S. lithium company. LIT had a 7.63% exposure to ALB on March 20, 2025. On March 5, 2026, LIT’s ALB exposure edged lower to 7.14%, but Rio Tinto (RIO) replaced ALB as LIT’s top holding with a 22.05% exposure. In late May 2025, I wrote, “The joint venture could eventually lead to RIO’s inclusion in the LIT ETF product.”

Higher Lows With High Volatility

The LIT ETF has been very volatile over the past few years.

The 20-year monthly chart highlights the LIT ETF's extreme volatility. After falling to a low of $17.83 per share in March 2020 when the global pandemic gripped markets, LIT rallied to a record high of $97.13 per share in November 2021, where it ran out of upside steam. LIT corrected 67.6% to a low of $31.44 in April 2025, where it found a bottom. The latest 148.1% rally took LIT to a high of $78 per share in February 2026. LIT has backed off from the recent high and was trading above $68 per share on March 5, 2026.

The monthly chart shows LIT remains in a bullish pattern, making higher lows since 2015, and while it made a lower high in February 2026, the trajectory remains positive. Moreover, the anticipated deficit in the global lithium market supports higher share prices for LIT’s holdings over the coming years. LIT is a volatile ETF, as lithium is a volatile commodity.

LIT's Seeking Alpha ETF Grades Are Mixed

Seeking Alpha’s latest ETF grades for LIT are as follows:

The rally in LIT since the April 2025 low earns it the highest possible A+ grade in the momentum category. LIT’s assets under management and daily trading volume earn it an A- in the liquidity category. The 0.75% management fee receives a D+ grade in expenses, while the dividend yields earn a D. The high risk and volatility of lithium producer shares receive a D- in the risk category.

Given the rally from April 2025 to the February high, I have changed my buy recommendation to a hold recommendation. However, given the deficit, the prospects for rising lithium prices over the coming years justify the current hold recommendation.

data.title: The AES Corporation (AES) Stock Analysis: Riding The AI Wave | 2-Minute Analysis
data.description:
Follow The AES Corporation on Seeking Alpha!

Learn more about Seeking Alpha Quant Ratings

Get Started With Seeking Alpha Premium Now

This video's transcript was generated by a third party. It is not curated or reviewed and is provided for convenience and information purposes only. The accuracy and completeness of the transcript are not guaranteed.

Welcome to 2 Minute Analysis. Our goal is to not only entertain, but provide value and insights about the investments you care about. Today's episode is from this comment. So, let's throw 2 minutes on the clock and dive-in.

Today, we are diving into The AES Corporation, ticker symbol AES. Kicking things off here with the Quant rating system, it is currently a Hold rating on the stock, and we're going to dive into the Factor Grade metrics here in just a moment. Seeking Alpha Analysts currently have a Buy in aggregate on this stock, and that's from 2 analysts providing their coverage in the last 90-days.

Lastly, Wall Street analysts currently have a Buy on this stock as well, and that's from 14 analysts providing their coverage in the last 90-days. To learn more about how the Seeking Alpha Quant system and Seeking Alpha Analysts outperform the market, visit the link in the description of this video.

Now, let's dive deeper. This is currently a $12.31 billion market capitalization company found within the utility sector and the independent power producers and energy traders industry. First things first, jumping into this Valuation grade, which is currently an A, we can see that the PEG non-GAAP forward ratio is currently 0.83, compared to the sector at 2.66, showcasing almost a 68.5% discount to the sector.

Jumping into the Growth grade currently a D-, that EPS GAAP growth forward is currently sitting at 79.17%, compared to the sector at just 7.89%. Also want to highlight the operating cash flow growth year-over-year is 63.50%, compared to the sector at just 4.14%. Very favorable metric there.

Jumping into the Profitability is currently a C grade. We can look at a return on common equity sitting at 30.16%, compared to the sector at just 9.36%. And they do have a nice cash from operations over the trailing 12-months of 3.91 billion, compared to the sector at just 1.44 billion.

Jumping into the Momentum grade that is currently an A+, pretty favorable across the board here, obviously, with this company being within the power producers industry, 66.47% compared to the sector at just 24.06% riding the AI wave.

Jumping into the Revisions grade, currently a C-. That is 3 up revisions and 3 down revisions for earnings per share over the last three months, and 3 up revisions and 1 down revision for revenue numbers over the last three months.

Now that this is a utility, it does pay a dividend. You will see the dividend yield is 4.07%. Dividend safety grade is currently a B-. The dividend growth is a D. The dividend yield is an A grade, and the dividend consistency is a B-.

Jumping in here, we can see over consistency, they have been paying their dividend for 13 years and growing it for 12 years. And if we take a quick look at the dividend yield, it is currently beating its sector on that yield, which is nice and favorable for shareholders.

Obviously, want to highlight as well, AES Corporation is taking a little bit of a hit today. So, if you want to get all the free breaking news alerts about what's going on with this company right now, come here to Seeking Alpha. Click this follow button here at the top of the page. You'll get free breaking news alerts on The AES Corporation and any other symbol that you want to follow here on Seeking Alpha.

And that's going to wrap it up for this episode. If you have a ticker you want us to cover, add it to the comment section below, and don't forget to follow Seeking Alpha so you get notified when the next 2 Minute Analysis gets published.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Content provided for informational purposes only. Seeking Alpha does not offer any personalized investment advice and is not a licensed securities dealer, broker, US investment adviser, or investment bank.

data.title: FNDA: Small Value ETF Beating The Benchmark But Lagging Some Peers
data.description:
This article updates my review of September 2025 in light of current holdings and recent performance.

FNDA strategy

Schwab Fundamental US Small Company ETF (FNDA) was launched on 08/15/2013 and tracks the RAFI Fundamental High Liquidity U.S. Small Index. FNDA has 889 holdings, a 30-day SEC yield of 1.24%, and an expense ratio of 0.25%. Distributions are paid quarterly. FNDA is part of a series of ETFs based on the RAFI methodology, also including:

  • Schwab Fundamental US Broad Market Index ETF (FNDB)
  • Schwab Fundamental US Large Company Index ETF (FNDX)
  • Schwab Fundamental International Equity ETF (FNDF)
  • Schwab Fundamental International Small Equity ETF (FNDC)
  • Schwab Fundamental Emerging Markets Equity ETF (FNDE)

As described in the prospectus by Charles Schwab Asset Management:

The index selects, ranks, and weights securities by fundamental measures of company size – adjusted sales, retained operating cash flow, and dividends plus buybacks – rather than market capitalization.

This methodology based on a fundamental size score is common to all RAFI indexes. The starting universe is composed of the U.S. companies in the RAFI Global Equity Investable Universe. The bottom 12.5% of the companies by cumulative score are included in the underlying index. The index is divided into four equal segments rebalanced quarterly, resulting in an annual complete rebalancing. The portfolio’s turnover rate was 26% in the most recent fiscal year. I will use as a benchmark the Russell 2000 index, represented by iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM).

Portfolio

The fund is diversified across sectors, with notable exposure in industrials (20.5% of asset value), financials (16.5%), consumer discretionary (13.6%), and technology (11.4%). Other sectors are below 10%. Compared to the Russell 2000, FNDA greatly downplays healthcare.

The portfolio has low company-specific risk. The top 10 issuers, listed in the next table, represent only 5.2% of asset value, and the heaviest position weighs about 1%.

data.title: Amazon: Berkshire Hathaway Q4 Trimming And Capital Lease Accounting In Focus
data.description:
AMZN stock: FQ4 earnings met with BRK disclosure

I last covered Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) on January 31 with an article titled "Amazon Q4 2025 Earnings: How Bullwhip Pressures Inventory And Margin.” The article served as a preview for the earnings report (ER) the company filed for its 4th fiscal quarter of 2025 on Feb. 5 and rated the stock as a Hold. Since then, there have been a few new catalysts evolving the company.

In the rest of this follow-up, I will focus on two particular changes: the operation update provided in its actual FQ4 earnings report and also the position change disclosed by Berkshire Hathaway (BRK), which is often also interpreted by the investing community as a market event. After examining these developments, I keep seeing a mixed picture blending future profit growth with intensifying capital requirements and thus reiterate my hold rating.

Let me start with a brief recap of the FQ4 ER and my observations. As shown in the snapshot below, Amazon’s earnings in FQ4 2025 continued the consistent growth on both lines. The company concluded the year with a strong FQ4 2025 revenue of $213.39B, representing a double-digit 13.6% year-over-year (YOY) increase and a sizable $2.15B beat. Earnings per share grew to $1.95, a YOY growth of around 4.5% compared to the $1.85 it earned in FQ4 2024.

However, the picture is not all positive. For reasons detailed in my last article, the margin did suffer a noticeable contraction, as shown in the second chart. Net profit margin retreated to 9.93%, compared to about 12% in the past quarter (which is also the record in the past 3 years). More importantly, the ER also revealed surging CAPEX and shrinking free cash flow (FCF) in the near future, which is a key cause for BRK’s significant reduction of its AMZN position in my mind, as detailed next.

AMZN stock and BRK position changes

For readers not following BRK’s AMZN holdings, the chart below provides a brief (and abridged) summary. Berkshire Hathaway’s engagement with Amazon began in Q1 2019 with an initial purchase of 9.67 million shares at an average cost of approximately $89, as seen. This initial position underwent some relatively minor additions and trimmings over the years, but BRK’s AMZN position remained relatively stable until Q4 2025. As seen, a dramatic shift occurred in Q4 2025, when Berkshire reduced 77.2% of its AMZN position in a price range of around $230. As a patient holder with a long-term focus, AMZN was another success story for Berkshire, earning a return of over 158% in the past 6~7 years since the company initiated the position.

But BRK’s position changes also leave me wondering "why." Why did it view Amazon as a go-and-see play rather than a buy-and-forget holding like Coca-Cola (KO) or American Express (AXP)?

AMZN stock: free cash flow in focus

BRK’s initial purchase of Amazon in 2019 marks all the hallmarks of a buy-and-forget investment at that time in my model. The company combined all three core factors for such an investment: the dominance of its logistics moat, the proven profitability of AWS (which has been providing a utility-like free cash flow), and a fair valuation at that time. As a reflection of these traits, the company’s CAPEX has remained relatively low between 2019 and 2024.

As you can see from the following chart, quarterly CAPEX remained below $20 billion during that period and dropped to as low as around $10 billion during 2023. However, all of these have changed, and changes climaxed in Q4 2025 in my view. To start, the company had to drastically crank up its CAPEX expenditures in 2024 as the AI arms race began. CAPEX rose rapidly to more than $39 billion in the quarter, more than doubling the average of $19 billion in the past 5 years.

Even more alarmingly, the company announced a massive, AI-driven $200 billion capital expenditure plan for FY 2026. Per my estimate, with this plan, Amazon's free cash flow will turn negative in 2026. These changes have defeated the premise of AWS’ utility-like cash flow (and there might be no free cash flow to speak of at all), and the valuation of the stock has also come into question with the shrinking FCF.

As an example of the worsening valuation picture, the next chart illustrates the P/FCF ratio of the stock in the past 5 years. As seen with the normalization of its CAPEX and cash flow in 2023 and 2024, the stock has been trading with a P/FCF of around 35x during those years. With the rapid surge of the CAPEX and the diminishing FCF, the ratio has expanded drastically since then. With my above FCF outlook, the P/FCF ratio can get even worse going forward.

Other risks and final thoughts

At this point, some readers must have this counterargument ready for me already: but Amazon’s valuation ratios are quite reasonable in terms of P/E. Indeed, as you can see from the following chart, the stock is now trading at 28x FWD P/E, which is more or less around the midground among the Magnificent Seven stocks. When adjusted for growth, Amazon’s FWD PEG (on a non-GAAP basis) of 1.50 also paints a similar picture. Thus, it is certainly a valid argument that AZMN’s valuation is not as alarming as the P/FCF ratio suggests.

This is where I need to point out a more advanced accounting item most investors probably have ignored: capital leases. It’s an item that many companies use, and it shows up on the balance sheet (as you can see from the following screenshot for AMZN). What makes this item stand out for AMZN is A) the magnitude, and B) the consistency that AMZN uses it. As seen, AMZN’s capital leases are more than its long-term debts, and they have been rapidly increasing over the years. In a nutshell, by using capitalized leases at such scales, the accounting EPS could be an overestimate of the company’s earnings in my analysis. More details of this accounting item are provided in an earlier article. I will just quote the gist here for ease of reference.

Most of us get the impression that leases are one-time and non-reoccurring expenses, which is true in many cases (like leasing a moving truck only when we need it). However, Amazon's substantial lease obligations are primarily driven by its extensive logistical operations and infrastructure requirements. And its lease obligations are an integrated part of these vast networks now. Take its AWS as an example. AWS heavily relies on leased equipment for its infrastructure needs. As long as Amazon intends to maintain its AWS business, I see it as a necessity for AMZN to keep renewing or replacing the leased equipment when the lease agreements expire. As such, capitalizing these leases tends to make its accounting earnings better than the true economic earnings.

To close, this Amazon revisit was motivated by its actual FQ4 ER and also the Q4 disclosure provided by BRK. In particular, it focuses on the CAPEX, FCF, and use of capital leases as updated in the company’s ER. These developments paint a mixed picture about the stock’s return profile, blending conflicting factors such as growth potential, intensifying capital requirements, and valuation risks. With these considerations, my hold rating is reiterated.

data.title: SentinelOne: AI Is A Cybersecurity Prompt, Not A Threat
data.description:
SentinelOne, Inc. (S) heads into FQ4 earnings with the stock oddly not trading down much over the last week due to the AI replacement fears. The cybersecurity stock was already trading substantially lower over the last 6 months due to fears unexplained by the financial results. My investment thesis is ultra Bullish on SentinelOne, with signs some AI-related acquisitions are paying off.

AI Threats

Cybersecurity firms spent the last week explaining why the sector isn't going to be replaced by Claude Code. Heading into the week, cybersecurity was a sector expected to greatly benefit from the growth of AI and agentic AI to protect corporate platforms and data, but a lot of the leading stocks had produced big losses.

In fact, the stock crashes of sector leaders got so bad CrowdStrike (CRWD) CEO George Kurtz took to social media to explain the business wasn't going to be upended by vibe coding. Even Claude seems to suggest Anthropic isn't going to replace cybersecurity products.

As highlighted in the last earnings call, SentinelOne has now seen the business completely shift away from endpoint security. The cybersecurity company now has ~50% of quarterly bookings from other areas like data, Purple AI, and Cloud Security Services.

SentinelOne gets high scores for AI security via Purple AI and a new emerging threat in agentic AI via the acquisition of Prompt Security back in 2025. Software Analyst Cyber Research listed the company as a leader in Unified Agentic Defense Platforms (UADP), right behind the much larger Palo Alto Networks (PANW).

Even research from Oppenheimer provided indications that SentinelOne saw strong growth to start 2026 after ending 2025 on a strong note. The analyst noted the partner checks actually showed the business growing at a high 20% growth rate, with Purple AI beating the Charlotte AI solution from CrowdStrike.

Big Quarter Next Week

SentinelOne has seen the stock slump due to AI fears and a general lack of interest in the business despite strong growth rates. The company reported 23% growth in the last quarter and will report big FQ4 results next week.

For the January quarter, the cybersecurity company guided to revenues of $271 million, up ~20% YoY. SentinelOne will report results next week on March 12, and a good quarter, as forecast by Oppenheimer, should lead to another beat.

The market will naturally latch onto guidance for FY27. The consensus analyst estimates suggest 20% growth to $1.2 billion for the year, while Oppenheimer and AI demand would indicate higher growth rates.

Either way, SentinelOne isn't being valued at anywhere close to 20% growth. Even with the other cybersecurity stocks selling off, the stock still trades at a massive gap to CrowdStrike and Palo Alto Networks while generating similar, if not better, quarterly results.

SentinelOne is down to only 4x forward sales, while CrowdStrike still trades with 4x the forward P/S multiple. Even more insane, analysts have forecast upwards of 50% upside in the stock with price targets from $550 to $600, suggesting a forward P/S multiple of 25x, or over 6x the multiple of SentinelOne.

The company only forecasts a 5% operating margin in FQ4. The market likely wants to see higher margins, but SentinelOne just got a $1 billion ARR run rate, and substantial leverage can occur at this scale with the addition of $200+ million in new ARR on an annual basis.

SentinelOne had a cash balance of $874 million with a trailing-12-month free cash flow margin of 5%. The company isn't producing a ton of cash, but the business should continue generating leverage after a 400 basis point improvement YoY.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that SentinelOne just doesn't get much credit. The cybersecurity company has made some investments in the AI space with Prompt Security and Observo AI, while Purple AI is starting to gain solid traction. While the stock isn't getting much respect, the upcoming FQ4 results could provide a major catalyst for the beaten-down stock with more confirmation of 20% growth, possibly altering the trajectory of the stock.

data.title: Delinquency Rates Fall Again
data.description:
In the credit markets it is not about how much debt there is but how well we are paying for it that matters. And in a consumer economy, it is consumer delinquencies which matter most. If consumers are comfortable handling debt i.e., credit cards and consumer loans, then lenders will keep lending. Once lenders fear consumer defaults, the lending can nearly cease till lenders reassess, and tighten lending standards before extending additional credit.

Credit Card Debt and Consumer Loans are both trending lower and well below their respective economic levels that signaled fragile consumer finances in the past.

In the US, consumers are healthy financially and economic expansion is expected to continue.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Examining Historical CLO Equity Returns With ECC
data.description:
"How a Deluge of Downgrades Could Sink the CLO Market"

"Will the Leveraged Loan Market Trigger a Financial Pandemic?"

"The Coming CLO Meltdown"

It's no secret that CLO equity funds have had a horrendous time lately. Distributions have been cut, NAVs have deteriorated, and share prices have fallen sharply across much of the sector. And the scary headlines certainly aren't helping.

When prices fall like this, the conversation often shifts from analysis to hindsight. The bears and the "told you so" crowd come out in full force: "CLOs are uninvestable," "the asset class is doomed," or even "they're all going to zero!" We heard very similar critiques around CLO funds during the depths of the 2020 crash, but in hindsight, that period turned out to be one of the best buying opportunities for these funds.

Oh, and one more little detail: those scary headlines above were from 2020, not 2026.

To be clear, I'm not claiming that history must repeat itself, nor that the current bear market in these funds will resolve in the same way.

At the Income Lab, we're neither bashers nor cheerleaders. We try to examine the data and understand the risks, without pretending we can forecast market turning points on demand.

Recency bias is powerful, and even professional analysts often end up following price trends rather than forecasting them. In practice, a lot of the "analysis" is backwards looking. When prices rise, analyst targets mysteriously float higher, but when prices fall, the same analysts discover new reasons to cut targets. The simple reality is that market timing is impossible to do consistently, and this includes forecasting NAV movements of CLO equity funds. This is because markets are broadly efficient: they incorporate all currently known information, including consensus expectations of future prices.

CLO refresher

As a refresher for our newer readers, CLOs are a type of collateralized financial product backed by a portfolio of senior loans (also known as bank loans or leveraged loans), which are loans made to non-investment grade companies and secured by collateral. Senior loans are typically considered safer than unsecured high-yield bonds as they sit senior to unsecured debt in the event of default.

The CLO issues two main types of investor securities: CLO debt and CLO equity. CLO debt is divided into tranches with varying levels of risk and return, ranked by seniority. Senior debt tranches (e.g. AAA-rated) offer lower yields but receive priority for interest and principal payments. Lower rated (e.g. BB or B-rated) tranches carry higher credit risk than senior tranches but offer higher yields to compensate. Additionally, CLO debt is floating rate, meaning its interest payments adjust with changes in benchmark rates, such as SOFR, providing a hedge against rising interest rates. CLO debt investors are also protected by the "waterfall" structure, where lower-rated tranches absorb losses first before more senior tranches are impacted.

CLO equity is the riskiest part of the structure and sits at the bottom of the payment waterfall. Equity investors are paid only after all debt tranches have received their scheduled payments, which also means that they absorb the first losses if loans in the portfolio default. However, CLO equity also offers the highest potential returns, as investors receive all remaining cash flows after covering the debt tranches.

Retail investors generally cannot directly invest in individual CLOs, but a multitude of exchange-traded funds (ETFs) and CEFs are available to access this market segment. For the more liquid CLO debt tranches, an ETF structure is appropriate, as changes in investor demand can be accommodated easily through the purchase or redemption of shares. However, the less liquid CLO equity tranches are more suited for a closed-end fund structure, as this eliminates the need for daily redemptions, allowing fund managers to hold these illiquid assets without being forced to sell them under unfavorable market conditions.

Historical CLO equity returns

A challenge with understanding CLO equity is that it does not have a widely followed index in the way that equities or most fixed income classes, including CLO debt, do.

A useful reference point for CLO equity is this Nomura chart showing median CLO equity returns over the past 13 years. The annual dispersion is fairly wide, with strong years (nearly +50% in 2016) and weak years (around -20% in 2015 and 2022). 2025 was also poor, with a median CLO equity return of around -15%.

At this point, it's important to remember what CLO equity actually is. Despite often residing in "income" portfolios, CLO equity is, well, equity, not fixed income. It is effectively an arbitrage on the spread between what the CLO earns on its loan assets and what it pays on its CLO debt liabilities. This arbitrage is what can support CLO equity's cash distributions. But due to its inherently leveraged structure as well as its first-loss position at the bottom of the capital stack, the equity's NAV can move violently up or down in a single year depending on spreads, defaults, as well as other input parameters.

This is also why we've always been direct with members about what CLO equity can, and can't, deliver. The cash distribution is not the same thing as the expected total return, and it should not be treated as a guaranteed fixed income yield. As we discussed in Why CLO Equity Could Be In For More Pain Ahead (And How To Navigate It):

It's also important to be realistic with the total return potential of CLO CEFs, which often yield in excess of 20%. A significant portion of the high cash flows from CLO equity comes from the repayment of principal, meaning that such high yields shouldn't be considered completely sustainable over the long term.

So what is the actual total return potential of CLO equity? In the Nomura chart above, the average of the annual median returns of CLO equity over the last 13 years has been around 8.8% (an approximate number based on eyeballing values from the chart).

Hence, any CLO equity CEF's performance should be viewed in light of the average CLO equity experience. If a fund primarily owns CLO equity, its NAV total return will generally move up and down with CLO equity's ups and downs. Expecting continued, year-after-year deviation from the underlying asset's return is simply not a realistic standard.

We're not cheerleaders of CLO funds, but when emotions and sentiment take over, it's even more important to step back and look at the data rather than headlines or hindsight narratives.

If CLO equity has returned 8.8% annually, why have CLO funds disappointed?

An 8.8% long-run average for an asset class as volatile as CLO equity may or may not fit your objectives. However, many investors in CLO equity funds haven't even experienced returns that look like that. The disconnect is largely explained by how the closed-end funds trade (at premiums or discounts), not necessarily by the underlying CLO equity asset class "failing".

Take Eagle Point Credit (ECC) as an example, the oldest publicly traded CLO equity CEF that was launched in 2014. ECC presents its inception price return at +5.13% annualized, which at first glance is not very impressive relative to the underlying senior loan market. Leveraged loans produced +4.98% annualized over a similar horizon, but with much lower volatility.

On YCharts, ECC's 10-year total price return is shown as +56.61%, which is actually even lower than Invesco Senior Loan ETF's (BKLN) roughly +60.10% return, which was again delivered with much less drama.

As an interesting aside, as recently as late 2024, ECC's compounded total return was around +160%, roughly triple BKLN's return at the time. Yet, you didn't hear many proclamations then about how "uninvestable" CLO equity supposedly was. The reason is straightforward: ECC spent much of 2022 and 2023 trading at a large premium to NAV (at times approaching 30%), which tends to mute criticism as the price action remained robust. Although we held small positions in ECC at the time (but also swapped out of it when it became too expensive), we weren't cheerleading it either. It's a simple reminder of how often investor narratives often follow price action, rather than lead it.

Using Morningstar's data, ECC's 10-year price return presents at +4.96% annualized, which is not dissimilar to ECC's stated inception price return of +5.13%.

Taken at face value, the price return performance of ECC has undeniably been disappointing. But it can also be misleading, because many investors stop at the market price chart, build a narrative from it, and never look through to see what the underlying NAV actually did.

The real driver of "disappointing" returns: CEF premium/discount fluctuations

Let's now turn to the NAV data to uncover what's really going on. Remember, it's the NAV returns that reflect the performance of the underlying portfolio, as it's not distorted by investors' often fickle behavior: pushing CEFs to premiums when times are good, but driving them to wide discounts during times of panic.

While ECC doesn't present a long-run NAV total return figure, Morningstar does. And here, ECC's 10-year annualized NAV total return comes in at a respectable +10.26%, broadly consistent with the Nomura's median CLO equity performance data above.

So why is there such a huge gap between ECC's price returns (~5% annualized) and NAV returns (~10% annualized)? The main reason is valuation. ECC moved to a massive premium not long after inception, reaching approximately +40% premium in early 2016, which also happens to be right around the starting point for today's 10-year lookback window. When a fund begins a period at a large +40% premium and then ends it at a roughly -26% discount, the total price return will inevitably look far, far worse than the underlying NAV experience.

Compounding this issue is the mechanics of dividend reinvestment, an effect that's amplified for ECC as well as other CLO equity funds because of their high distribution yields. For long stretches of its history, ECC traded at a premium to NAV. Total price return calculations generally assume that distributions are reinvested at the market price; when a fund trades at a premium, that reinvestment is effectively done at an expensive valuation. This "reinvestment penalty" is a significant headwind to long-run compounding and can make reported price total return look much worse than the underlying NAV total return, especially if the premium later flips to a discount. Obviously, you can't reinvest at NAV when the fund is priced above it, but our solution has always been straightforward: avoid systematic reinvestment at large premiums and rotate into comparable CEFs trading at discounts when available.

When we put ECC's price and NAV total return profiles side by side, we can see just how much of an effect a premium/discount swing of this magnitude can be. Over the last 10 years through November 2025 (YCharts' NAV data for ECC only goes up to then), ECC's NAV total return is roughly +190%, versus a price total return of only +110%, a massive 80 percentage point difference.

If YCharts extended its NAV total return series through January, the 10-year NAV total return of ECC would be roughly 178% after accounting for NAV decline and two distributions paid. Against ECC's current 10-year price total return of about +47%, the gap is even more dramatic, a staggering 131 percentage points. It's a stark illustration that in CEFs, premium/discount swings can matter as much as, or even more than, the actual portfolio performance.

Let's now compare ECC's year-by-year NAV returns to the Nomura data to take an even more granular look at the numbers. I used Morningstar's annual total return NAV numbers here, but I also cross-checked these with manual calculations (ending NAV plus distributions, divided by starting NAV), and got similar results.

And lo and behold, ECC's NAV performance each year was broadly similar to the median CLO equity experience.

When CLO equity was up, ECC was up. When CLO equity was down, ECC was down. Notably, while there were some differences in yearly performance, there were exactly zero years where both CLO equity was up and ECC down, or CLO equity down and ECC up.

And why shouldn't it be this way? ECC largely invests in CLO equity. Expecting them to deviate from the underlying asset's return is not sensible.

Thus, when CLO equity returned -15% in 2025, we shouldn't expect ECC to be able to continue paying out +30% distribution yields indefinitely. At this point, a distribution cut isn't just possible - it's prudent, because this avoids having to return capital and erode NAV.

What's also interesting to note is that ECC actually performed even worse at the NAV level in 2022 (-15.8%) than last year (-14.0%). Yet the detractors were much quieter back then; likely because ECC traded at a healthy premium, so the price action still looked robust. It's another demonstration of how market price often shapes the narrative, feeding a kind of self-fulfilling loop: rising prices attract praise, while falling prices invite scorn, even when the underlying NAV story is similar.

To reiterate, we're not cheerleaders for ECC or any other CLO fund. But the data's the data.

How CEF rotation can help cushion losses and deliver gains

Academic research suggests that CEF discounts are mean-reverting, because investors tend to overreact in both directions: bidding funds to premiums in good times and driving them to deep discounts in bad times. This means that astute closed-end fund investors can stack the odds in their favor by buying CEFs when discounts are unusually wide (such as where they are now) and reducing or rotating out of CEFs when they return to or rise above fair value.

That valuation gap, between perception (price) and reality (NAV), is where opportunity can exist for contrarian CEF investors. While there are no guarantees that discounts will revert on any specific time frame or even at all, focusing on relative value has historically been our most reliable source of alpha in this space.

Of course, as with any contrarian strategy, this approach requires an iron stomach. When NAVs fall and discounts widen at the same time, price declines compound and drawdowns can be severe. However, the potential rewards can be substantial. Our unique CEF rotation strategy is designed to harvest changes in relative valuation, and not to depend on precise market forecasts or require rising NAVs. Crucially, rotation can still work even when an entire sector is under pressure.

Indeed, our most profitable CLO equity trades have come during highly volatile periods, as we've previously shown in How CEF Rotation Can Turn Losers Into Winners. For example, rotating between the ECC and Oxford Lane Capital Corp (OXLC) rotation pair generated significant profits for our Income Generator portfolio during the turbulent March-June 2020 period, far outperforming buy-and-hold of either OXLC or ECC, as well as the S&P 500 ETF (SPY).

In the 2022 bear market two years later, rotation in the ECC/OXLC pair again boosted our results, delivering higher total returns than a buy-and-hold approach in either fund, and outperforming SPY as well:

Our income grew alongside the growth of share counts:

In terms of raw numbers, I also tallied the cumulative total return from all CLO positions in the Income Generator portfolio, including both closed and currently open positions. Since the portfolio's inception in 2017 (started with $100,000 and no additional capital added), historical ECC trades have contributed $19,123 in returns, while OXLC positions have contributed $6,917 in total returns (comprised of $10,210 from closed trades and a loss of -$3,294 on the currently open position).

It's tricky to calculate a simple IRR number because position sizes varied over time, but my estimate is that our largest combined exposure to the ECC/OXLC pair was roughly 10% of the portfolio (about $20,000). On that basis, roughly $25,000 of cumulative gains from ECC and OXLC translates into more than a doubling of the capital we had allocated at peak exposure. So when some investors claim you that can't make money in these funds, our portfolio results beg to differ!

The same premium/discount dynamics show up clearly in other funds as well. XAI Octagon Floating Rate & Alternative Income Trust's (XFLT), another current holding of the Income Generator portfolio, holds a diversified mix of CLO equity, CLO debt and senior loans. Over the last five years, XFLT exhibited a total price return of negative -19.40%, while its NAV total return is +19.40% - the exact same number but with the sign flipped. A perfect mirror symmetry that would be beautifully elegant if it weren't so painful for shareholders. In effect, the premium/discount swing imposed a roughly 40-point headwind on market price results, turning a positive underlying portfolio performance into a significant loss for shareholders. A 40% drag from premium/discount variance is very hard to overcome, it's really just as simple as that.

Closing thoughts

Hopefully, readers can see that the data presented above points to a few straightforward conclusions. First, CLO equity returns have always been cyclical and are more equity-like than fixed-income-like, and CLO equity CEF NAVs generally move with those underlying cycles. Second, the biggest disconnect in many investor experiences hasn't been NAV behavior - it's been large premium/discount swings of the CEFs that can turn decent underlying portfolio performances into disappointing price outcomes. Third, narratives in this space often track price action more than fundamentals: when prices rise, criticism dims; but when prices fall sharply, the sector is declared uninvestable.

A core point of the Income Lab philosophy is that no one can time markets consistently, and outcomes often look obvious only in hindsight, including what's unfolding in the CLO equity space today. Again, this isn't an excuse or justification, it's just the reality of a highly efficient market.

Are CLOs the perfect asset class? No, and they're not for everyone. CLO equity is inherently volatile, and CLO equity CEFs add another layer of complexity through fund leverage and premium/discount fluctuations. This explosive combination can be disconcerting and even downright painful at times, but it also creates the volatility that nimble CEF investors, including ourselves, have historically been able to take advantage of.

That's also why our portfolios are centered around diversification and risk management, rather than relying on having to make accurate macro calls or sector predictions. CLO equity is just one component of a broader portfolio, and we fully expect that not every position will outperform in every time frame. The objective is to manage risk at the portfolio level so weakness in one area can be balanced by strength elsewhere. For example, falling rates have pressured floating-rate assets (including CLOs), but our fixed-rate positions have performed much better in the same environment (see What Recent Distribution Cuts Mean, And Why I'm Not Concerned). Our tracked portfolio results reflect this: both income and total return have compounded substantially since inception nearly a decade ago.

Are we perfect in our calls? Of course not. In hindsight, should we have trimmed more aggressively when some of these funds rose to even modest premiums (say 5%)? Of course. We're not claiming to be infallible: investing is an ongoing learning process to review what worked, what didn't, and refining methodologies to improve outcomes for next time.

But investing by looking in the rear-view mirror isn't a value-oriented process. The more useful question is what today's valuations imply going forward: all else being equal, cheaper prices and wider discounts raise future expected returns, even if the recent price chart looks ugly.

This concludes Part 1 of the series, where we examine historical CLO equity returns and how CLO equity funds have behaved relative to their underlying asset class. The goal was to tackle some of the loudest critiques in context, while also showing how our CEF rotation strategy has been able to generate returns even in difficult market environments.

In Part 2, we'll survey some of the difficulties currently faced by CLO equity and also ask whether the landscape has materially changed going forward.

In Part 3, we'll take a critical look at whether CEFs are the best vehicle for gaining exposure to these complex instruments.

Addendum: ECC's historical NAV total return performance has been bolstered by its ability to issue new shares at a premium, which is accretive to NAV per share. Hence, it can be argued that had ECC not spent so much time at a premium, it would not have received this "boost" from accretive share issuance, and thus its NAV total return should be adjusted to better isolate its underlying portfolio's actual performance. Using data provided from ECC's annual reports, I estimate that NAV accretion added around +1.8% per year to ECC's NAV total returns. For completeness, I present ECC's annual NAV total return numbers after adjusting for accretion. Nevertheless, the chief conclusions of this article remain broadly unchanged.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
